content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                          Date : Feb. 9, 1977

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ken Finney

Subject: Telephone Conversation with Charlene Bomar, Assistant City
         Attorney for Modesto.

In a telephone conversation with Charlene Bomar, a number of
topics were discussed. First, I informed her that a City
Manager who files pursuant to Section 87200 need not file a
disclosure statement pursuant to the Conflict of Interest
Code of the City of Modesto. The City Manager should be
included in the code for purposes of disqualification and
in order to make additional sanctions applicable to him with
respect to disqualification, but he does not have to file
another disclosure statement. Ms. Bomar pointed out that
this can mean that certain lesser officials in the city will
have a broader disclosure obligation than the City Manager
because in addition to disclosing everything required by
Section 87200 (based on the notion that their duties are
broad and indefinable) these persons also disclose manage-
ment positions in certain business entities. On the other
hand, the City Manager does not have to disclose management
positions. I agreed with her that this is anomalous but
pointed out that at present it is the Commission's policy
not to require an additional disclosure statement. This
is based on the provision in Section 87302(b) which states
that any designated employee covered by Article 2 shall not
have to file a disclosure statement under a Code.

Ms. Bomar and I then discussed the situation of the Planning
Commissioner, which my memorandum of January 28, 1977, also
discusses. I informed Ms. Bomar that the staff is split
with respect to the question of whether the Planning Com-
missioner would have to disqualify himself when there was
not a material financial effect on the Union itself but
there would be a material financial effect on individual
members of the Union. I indicated to her that this raises
a substantial question and that in order to resolve it, it
would be necessary to have a formal opinion request. I
pointed out that the issue would also be involved in

File
P.2
Feb. 9, 1977

situations where you have an association and association
members or the Bar and the individual attorney members of
the Bar. Similar to the situation involving the Union Of-
ficer and the individual members of the Union, the issue
will arise of when the Union officer who is serving as a
public official must disqualify himself. In any event, Ms.
Bomar indicated that she would attempt to persuade the City
Attorney and the Planning Commissioner to seek a formal
opinion and I encouraged her to do so.

Finally, we discussed a number of subsidiary issues related
to the correct interpretation of Government Code Sections
81005 and 81009.

KF:jo

sections.question_synthetic: The document discusses multiple legal questions including whether a City Manager filing under Section 87200 must also file a disclosure statement under the City's Conflict of Interest Code, and whether a Planning Commissioner must disqualify themselves when there is a material financial effect on union members (but not the union itself).
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC staff's position is that a City Manager need not file an additional disclosure statement under the Conflict of Interest Code (based on Section 87302(b)), though this creates an anomalous situation where lesser officials have broader disclosure obligations. Regarding the Planning Commissioner's disqualification obligations when union members (but not the union) are materially affected, the staff is split and a formal opinion request is needed to resolve this substantial question.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal memorandum/correspondence rather than a formal advice letter response. No formal Q/A sections present. Multiple legal questions discussed informally. Staff appears split on planning commissioner disqualification issue; formal opinion request encouraged but not yet submitted.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The document discusses multiple legal questions including whether a City Manager filing under Section 87200 must also file a disclosure statement under the City's Conflict of Interest Code, and whether a Planning Commissioner must disqualify themselves when there is a material financial effect on union members (but not the union itself).

CONCLUSION: The FPPC staff's position is that a City Manager need not file an additional disclosure statement under the Conflict of Interest Code (based on Section 87302(b)), though this creates an anomalous situation where lesser officials have broader disclosure obligations. Regarding the Planning Commissioner's disqualification obligations when union members (but not the union) are materially affected, the staff is split and a formal opinion request is needed to resolve this substantial question.

In a telephone conversation with Charlene Bomar, a number of topics were discussed. First, I informed her that a City Manager who files pursuant to Section 87200 need not file a disclosure statement pursuant to the Conflict of Interest Code of the City of Modesto. The City Manager should be included in the code for purposes of disqualification and in order to make additional sanctions applicable to him with respect to disqualification, but he does not have to file another disclosure statement. Ms. Bomar pointed out that this can mean that certain lesser officials in the city will have a broader disclosure obligation than the City Manager because in addition to disclosing everything required by Section 87200 (based on the notion that their duties are broad and indefinable) these persons also disclose manage- ment positions in certain business entities. On the other hand, the City Manager does not have to disclose management positions. I agreed with her that this is anomalous but pointed out that at present it is the Commission's policy not to require an additional disclosure statement. This is based on the provision in Section 87302(b) which states that any designated employee covered by Article 2 shall not have to file a disclosure statement under a Code. Ms. Bomar and I then discussed the situation of the Planning Commissioner, which my memorandum of January 28, 1977, also discusses. I informed Ms. Bomar that the staff is split with respect to the question of whether the Planning Com- missioner would have to disqualify himself when there was not a material financial effect on the Union itself but there would be a material financial effect on individual members of the Union. I indicated to her that this raises a substantial question and that in order to resolve it, it would be necessary to have a formal opinion request. I pointed out that the issue would also be involved in File P.2 Feb. 9, 1977 situations where you have an association and association members or the Bar and the individual attorney members of the Bar. Similar to the situation involving the Union Of- ficer and the individual members of the Union, the issue will arise of when the Union officer who is serving as a public official must disqualify himself. Finally, we discussed a number of subsidiary issues related to the correct interpretation of Government Code Sections 81005 and 81009. KF:jo
embedding.summary: Internal FPPC memorandum documenting a telephone conversation between FPPC staff and Modesto's Assistant City Attorney regarding disclosure and disqualification requirements for city officials, including City Managers and Planning Commissioners under the Conflict of Interest Code.
